description: The Goethals-Seidel (GS) array constructs a Hadamard matrix of order 4v from four supplementary difference sets (SDS) over Z_v. For v=167 (a prime congruent to 3 mod 8), we need four blocks X0..X3 in Z_167 satisfying the constant autocorrelation-sum condition. The core computational challenge is searching for these four blocks using hashing-based matching algorithms, as pioneered by Dokovic, Golubitsky, and Kotsireas.
mathematical_formalization: Find X_0, X_1, X_2, X_3 subsets of Z_{167} with |X_i| = k_i such that sum_{i=0}^{3} k_i = 167 + lambda and for all g != 0 in Z_{167}: sum_{i=0}^{3} |{(x,y) in X_i x X_i : x - y = g}| = lambda. The GS array then yields H of order 668.
analogical_connections: SDS search is analogous to 4-dimensional sphere packing: find 4 subsets whose intersection patterns are uniformly distributed
The hashing-based matching resembles meet-in-the-middle attacks in cryptanalysis
The autocorrelation condition mirrors the flat spectral property of m-sequences in signal processing
implementation_hypothesis: Split the four blocks into two pairs (X0,X1) and (X2,X3). For each pair, enumerate candidates satisfying partial autocorrelation constraints, hash them by their partial autocorrelation spectrum, then match pairs whose combined spectra yield the constant lambda. Use orbit reduction under the automorphism group Aut(Z_167) = Z_166 to prune the search space by factor ~166.
experiment_seed: Implement a parallel search in Python/C++ over Z_167 with orbit reduction modulo the cyclic automorphism group. Start with symmetric or skew blocks to reduce search space. Measure: number of valid SDS found vs. CPU hours.

description: Cocyclic Hadamard matrices are developed from 2-cocycles over a group G, as introduced by Horadam and de Launey (1993). For order 668, we seek a group G of order 668 and a cocycle psi: GxG -> {+/-1} such that the cocyclic matrix M[g,h] = psi(g,h) is Hadamard. This algebraic approach transforms the search from a brute-force combinatorial problem into one of group cohomology computation.
mathematical_formalization: Given G with |G| = 668, compute H^2(G, Z_2). For each cohomology class [psi], form M_{g,h} = psi(g,h). Test orthogonality: M M^T = 668 I. Key groups: D_{4*167} (dihedral), Z_4 x Z_167, Z_668.
analogical_connections: Cocycles are the algebraic analog of gauge transformations in physics: they describe twisted symmetries
The cohomological approach mirrors how topological invariants classify knots -- classifying Hadamard matrices by their algebraic structure
Extension classes in group cohomology parallel fiber bundles in differential geometry
implementation_hypothesis: Use GAP or SageMath to enumerate H^2(G, Z_2) for candidate groups of order 668. For each coboundary class, construct the cocyclic matrix and test orthogonality. Exploit the dihedral group D_{4t} with t=167, known to produce many cocyclic Hadamard matrices. Use the genetic algorithm of Alvarez et al. as a guided search within each cohomology class.
experiment_seed: Compute H^2(D_{668}, Z_2) using GAP's cohomology package. For each representative cocycle, construct the 668x668 matrix and check row orthogonality. Profile which cohomology classes come closest to orthogonality.

description: Formulate Hadamard matrix search as a Quadratic Unconstrained Binary Optimization (QUBO) problem and solve via quantum annealing. Suksmono (2018, 2019) demonstrated this approach for small orders on D-Wave. For order 668, the challenge is encoding the orthogonality constraint H*H^T = 668*I as a penalty Hamiltonian with manageable connectivity, requiring reduction from quartic to quadratic terms via auxiliary variables.
mathematical_formalization: Let h_{ij} in {-1,+1} mapped to q_{ij} = (1-h_{ij})/2 in {0,1}. Minimize H_penalty = sum_{r<s} (sum_k h_{rk}*h_{sk})^2 = sum_{r<s} (sum_k (1-2q_{rk})(1-2q_{sk}))^2. This is naturally quartic; reduce to QUBO via auxiliary variables. Total variables: O(n^2) = O(668^2) ~ 446,224.
analogical_connections: The QUBO formulation is equivalent to finding the ground state of an Ising spin glass -- connecting combinatorics to statistical physics
Quantum tunneling through energy barriers is analogous to escaping local optima in classical simulated annealing
The penalty Hamiltonian structure mirrors constraint enforcement in protein folding energy landscapes
implementation_hypothesis: Use simulated quantum annealing (SQA) with transverse-field Ising model. Decompose the 668x668 problem into overlapping subproblems of size ~40x668 using a sliding-window approach. Each window fixes some rows and optimizes the rest. Iterate until convergence. This avoids the O(n^4) qubit requirement of the full QUBO.
experiment_seed: Implement SQA for the row-orthogonality Hamiltonian on subproblems. Start from a random +/-1 matrix and anneal in windows of 20 rows. Measure: residual off-diagonal norm of H*H^T after each sweep.

description: Genetic algorithms (GA) have been applied to Hadamard matrix construction, notably by Kotsireas and Koukouvinos (2005) for two-circulant-core designs, and by Ruiz (2022) with GPU acceleration. For order 668 = 4x167, encode four circulant first-rows (each a binary vector in Z_167) as chromosomes. Crossover and mutation operate on these compact representations, with fitness defined by closeness to the SDS autocorrelation condition.
mathematical_formalization: Chromosome: (x_0, x_1, x_2, x_3) in {0,1}^{4x167}. Fitness: f(x) = -sum_{g=1}^{166} (sum_{i=0}^{3} N_{X_i}(g) - lambda)^2, where N_{X_i}(g) = |{(a,b) in X_i^2 : a-b = g mod 167}|. Target: f = 0.
analogical_connections: Chromosomes encoding circulant first-rows are analogous to DNA encoding protein structure -- compact representations of complex objects
The fitness landscape of SDS conditions resembles NK fitness landscapes in evolutionary biology
GPU-parallel fitness evaluation mirrors massively parallel protein folding simulations
implementation_hypothesis: Encode each of the 4 blocks as a 167-bit chromosome. Use tournament selection, uniform crossover, and bit-flip mutation. Evaluate fitness via FFT-based autocorrelation (O(v log v) per block). Parallelize on GPU: each thread evaluates one individual. Population size: 10,000; generations: 100,000. Apply orbit-aware initialization: seed the population with blocks derived from cyclotomic classes.
experiment_seed: Run a GA with FFT-based fitness on a single GPU for 24 hours. Compare convergence rates for: (a) random initialization, (b) cyclotomic seeding, (c) Williamson-type symmetric constraint. Record best fitness vs. generation.

description: Model the Hadamard matrix construction as a constraint satisfaction problem encoded in a tensor network. Each entry h_{ij} is a binary tensor variable. Orthogonality constraints between rows become tensor contractions. The partition function of the network counts the number of valid Hadamard matrices; approximate contraction via belief propagation or DMRG-like sweeps can find solutions.
mathematical_formalization: Construct tensor T_{r,s,k} = delta(sum_k h_{rk}*h_{sk}, 0) for r != s. The full constraint tensor is T = prod_{r<s} T_{r,s}. Finding an assignment with T=1 is the Hadamard construction problem. Approximate via Matrix Product State (MPS) with bond dimension chi, sweeping over rows.
analogical_connections: Tensor network contraction for Hadamard construction parallels DMRG for ground state finding in 1D quantum systems
Bond dimension chi controls the approximation quality, analogous to rank in low-rank matrix approximation
The row-by-row sweep is analogous to Gaussian elimination -- progressively fixing variables
implementation_hypothesis: Represent each row of the 668x668 matrix as a tensor with indices for each column entry. Build a tensor network where contraction nodes enforce pairwise orthogonality. Use a DMRG-like sweep algorithm: optimize one row at a time while keeping others fixed in an MPS. Bond dimension starts at chi=2 and increases adaptively.
experiment_seed: Implement a toy version for order 28 (known Hadamard order) using ITensor library. Measure: (a) convergence to a valid Hadamard matrix, (b) bond dimension required, (c) scaling to order 44, 92. Extrapolate feasibility to 668.

description: Algebraic geometry (AG) codes over curves with many rational points produce generator matrices with strong orthogonality properties. A Hermitian curve over GF(q^2) yields codes close to the Singleton bound. By evaluating basis functions at rational points of a curve over GF(167^2) or a related field, one might construct a matrix with near-Hadamard properties that can be rounded or refined to a true Hadamard matrix.
mathematical_formalization: Let C be a curve over F_q with N rational points P_1,...,P_N. For a divisor G with deg(G) < N, the evaluation code C_L(G) = {(f(P_1),...,f(P_N)) : f in L(G)}. For q=167, the Hermitian curve y^q + y = x^{q+1} over F_{q^2} has q^3 + 1 rational points. Extract a 668x668 submatrix with maximal orthogonality.
analogical_connections: AG codes generalize Reed-Solomon codes just as Hadamard matrices generalize DFT matrices
The Riemann-Roch theorem guarantees code parameters, analogous to how the Hadamard bound guarantees matrix determinant
Evaluation at rational points is analogous to sampling a continuous function -- connecting algebraic geometry to signal processing
implementation_hypothesis: Use SageMath to construct the Hermitian curve over GF(167^2). Enumerate rational points. Construct an evaluation code of dimension 668. Extract the 668x668 generator matrix. Apply a rounding/sign procedure to map entries to {+/-1}. Measure the orthogonality defect and refine via local search.
experiment_seed: Construct Hermitian codes over GF(13^2) and GF(17^2) (smaller fields). Measure the orthogonality defect of the sign-rounded generator matrix. Determine if defect decreases with field size. Extrapolate to GF(167^2).

description: Treat the partially-constructed Hadamard matrix as a factor graph where variable nodes represent undetermined entries and factor nodes enforce pairwise row orthogonality. Apply belief propagation (BP) or survey propagation (SP) to iteratively refine entry assignments. This borrows from LDPC decoding, where BP efficiently solves similar constraint satisfaction problems on sparse graphs.
mathematical_formalization: Factor graph: variable nodes v_{ij} in {-1,+1}, factor nodes f_{rs} = delta(sum_k v_{rk}*v_{sk}, 0) for r<s. BP messages: mu_{v->f}(x) = prod_{f' in N(v)\f} mu_{f'->v}(x); mu_{f->v}(x) = sum_{x'} f(x,x') prod_{v' in N(f)\v} mu_{v'->f}(x'). Iterate until convergence.
analogical_connections: BP for Hadamard construction parallels turbo decoding -- iterative refinement with soft information
The factor graph is analogous to a Tanner graph for LDPC codes, with orthogonality constraints replacing parity checks
Survey propagation (from spin glass theory) handles frustrated loops, analogous to conflicting row constraints
implementation_hypothesis: Initialize a 668x668 matrix with a near-Hadamard solution (from partial construction or simulated annealing). For each violated orthogonality constraint, set up a BP subproblem over the involved rows. Run damped BP with momentum to handle cycles. Use decimation: fix high-confidence variables, re-run BP. Repeat until all constraints satisfied.
experiment_seed: Start with a random 668x668 +/-1 matrix. Run BP with decimation for 1000 iterations. Track: number of violated orthogonality constraints vs. iteration, convergence rate. Compare with direct SA baseline.

description: For v=167 (prime), the multiplicative group GF(167)* is cyclic of order 166 = 2x83. Cyclotomic classes of order N|166 partition GF(167)* into N cosets. Unions of these cyclotomic classes can form the blocks of supplementary difference sets. Karoui (2026) recently used cyclotomic unions over GF(5^4) to construct a skew-Hadamard matrix of order 1252. Adapting this to GF(167) with classes of order 2 or 83 could yield the needed SDS for H(668).
mathematical_formalization: Let g be a primitive root mod 167. The N-th cyclotomic classes are C_j = {g^{Nk+j} : k=0,...,166/N-1} for j=0,...,N-1. For N=2: C_0 = QR(167), C_1 = QNR(167), each of size 83. SDS blocks X_i are unions of C_j sets. The autocorrelation condition becomes equations on index sets S_i, solvable by exhaustive search over 2^N subsets.
analogical_connections: Cyclotomic classes are the number-theoretic analog of Fourier harmonics -- frequency components of the multiplicative group
Union assembly mirrors DNA recombination: combining genetic segments to build functional structures
The approach parallels wavelet construction: selecting frequency bands to achieve flatness
implementation_hypothesis: For 167, we have 166 = 2x83. Use cyclotomic classes of order 2 (QR and QNR). Each block is either QR, QNR, QR union {0}, or QNR union {0}. Check all combinations. For finer resolution, consider non-standard unions with additional algebraic structure from the field extension GF(167^2).
experiment_seed: Compute the Jacobsthal matrix J of GF(167). Form candidate SDS blocks as unions of cyclotomic classes for N=2. Check all 4-tuples (very small search space: ~16 combinations). If none work, extend to N=83 classes or use GF(167^2) structure.

description: Use deep reinforcement learning (RL) to guide the construction of supplementary difference sets for the Goethals-Seidel array. The RL agent sequentially adds elements to four blocks X_0,...,X_3, with state being the current partial blocks and reward derived from the autocorrelation defect. This combines structured search of traditional methods with learned heuristics of RL.
mathematical_formalization: MDP: State s_t = (X_0^{(t)}, X_1^{(t)}, X_2^{(t)}, X_3^{(t)}), partial subsets of Z_167. Action a_t = (i, g): add element g to block X_i. Reward r_t = -Delta(autocorrelation defect). Terminal reward: +1000 if valid SDS, 0 otherwise. Policy pi_theta(a|s) parameterized by a GNN on the Cayley graph of Z_167.
analogical_connections: Sequential block construction mirrors the game of Go: each element placement affects global balance of autocorrelations
The GNN on the Cayley graph captures algebraic structure of Z_167, analogous to how AlphaFold captures protein geometry
The sparse reward signal requires Monte Carlo tree search, paralleling game-playing AI
implementation_hypothesis: Model the Cayley graph of Z_167 as a graph with 167 nodes. The GNN processes current state and outputs a policy over (block_id, element) actions. Train with PPO using self-play. Use curriculum learning: start with smaller v (v=11, 23, 47) where solutions are known, then transfer to v=167.
experiment_seed: Train an RL agent to construct SDS for v=11 (order 44, known). Evaluate transfer to v=23 (order 92, known). Measure: success rate, episodes to convergence, quality of learned heuristics (do they discover cyclotomic structure?).

description: Although 668 cannot be directly decomposed as a Kronecker product of known Hadamard orders (since 668 = 4x167 and 167 is not a valid Hadamard order), a bootstrap approach constructs a near-Hadamard matrix H' of order 668 by starting from H_4 tensor M_167, where M_167 is derived from the Paley conference matrix of order 167 (which exists since 167 is an odd prime). Local refinement then corrects H' to achieve exact orthogonality.
mathematical_formalization: Let C_167 be the Paley conference matrix (167x167, C*C^T = 166*I). Construct M = [1 j^T; -j C] of order 168 (Paley Type I Hadamard). Extract a 167x167 submatrix. Form H' = H_4 tensor M'. Compute defect D = H'*H'^T - 668*I. Apply gradient-based or combinatorial repair to minimize ||D||_F by flipping entries.
analogical_connections: Bootstrapping from a conference matrix is like using a scaffold in 3D printing -- starting with approximate structure and refining
The Kronecker product structure acts like a coarse-grained model in molecular dynamics
Entry flipping to repair orthogonality mirrors bit-flipping decoding in LDPC codes
implementation_hypothesis: Step 1: Build 168x168 Paley Type I Hadamard from conference matrix of GF(167). Step 2: Delete row/column to get 167x167 near-orthogonal matrix M'. Step 3: Form H' = H_4 tensor M' (668x668). Step 4: Use simulated annealing to flip entries, minimizing Frobenius norm of H'H'^T - 668I. The initial structure dramatically reduces search space.
experiment_seed: Construct 168x168 Paley Hadamard. Delete row/col to get 167x167 matrix. Form H' = H_4 tensor M'. Measure initial defect. Run SA with entry-flipping for 10^8 iterations. Track defect convergence.

description: Map Hadamard matrix construction to a spin-glass energy minimization problem. Each entry h_{ij} in {-1,+1} is an Ising spin. Energy is the sum of squared inner products between distinct rows. Ground state (energy = 0) corresponds to a Hadamard matrix. Use replica-exchange Monte Carlo (parallel tempering) exploiting the physical analogy between orthogonality constraints and antiferromagnetic frustration.
mathematical_formalization: E(H) = sum_{r<s} (sum_{k=1}^{668} h_{rk} h_{sk})^2. Ground state: E=0 iff H is Hadamard. Parallel tempering: run K replicas at temperatures T_1 < T_2 < ... < T_K. Swap adjacent replicas with Metropolis acceptance.
analogical_connections: The Hadamard energy landscape is a spin glass with frustration -- connecting combinatorics to Edwards-Anderson models
Replica exchange is analogous to evolution with migration between populations at different selection pressures
Zero-energy ground states correspond to error-free codewords in coding theory
implementation_hypothesis: Run parallel tempering with 32 replicas at temperatures T_k = 0.1 * 1.2^k. Each replica is a 668x668 +/-1 matrix. Spin-flip updates: flip h_{ij} and compute delta_E in O(n) time. Exploit row structure: flipping h_{ij} only affects inner products involving row i. Total update cost per flip: O(668).
experiment_seed: Implement parallel tempering for E(H) on 668x668. Initialize with structured starts (Paley-like construction). Run for 24 hours on 32-core machine. Track: minimum energy found, replica exchange acceptance rates.

description: Adapt lattice sieving techniques from computational number theory (used in the Number Field Sieve for integer factoring) to enumerate supplementary difference sets. The SDS conditions define a lattice of valid block configurations in Z^{4x167}. Short vectors correspond to SDS with small block sizes. Lattice reduction (LLL/BKZ) followed by sieving can systematically enumerate candidate solutions.
mathematical_formalization: Define autocorrelation lattice L = {(x_0,...,x_3) in Z^{4x167} : forall g!=0, sum_i N_{supp(x_i)}(g) = lambda}. This is an affine sublattice of Z^{668}. Apply LLL reduction to find short vectors. Candidate SDS correspond to 0/1 vectors in L. Use CVP to find nearest 0/1 vector to a lattice point.
analogical_connections: Lattice sieving for SDS parallels lattice sieving for smooth values in NFS -- both seek structured elements in high-dimensional lattices
LLL reduction is analogous to Gram-Schmidt orthogonalization -- both find nearly orthogonal bases
The CVP formulation connects Hadamard matrix construction to lattice-based cryptography
implementation_hypothesis: Transform SDS autocorrelation conditions into a system of linear equations modulo suitable values. Construct the associated lattice. Apply BKZ-2.0 reduction. For each reduced basis vector, round to nearest 0/1 vector and check if it forms a valid SDS block. Use Schnorr-Euchner enumeration.
experiment_seed: Construct the autocorrelation lattice for v=23 (known SDS for order 92). Apply LLL and enumerate nearby 0/1 vectors. Verify known SDS solutions are found. Measure lattice dimension and gap. Scale to v=47, then v=167.
